query: evidence on circumcision in infancy including impact on penile cancer, UTIs, STIs, and recommendations from AAP CDC WHO

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance -- The American Academy of Pediatrics and the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists
retrieved_docs.content: In an update to the 1999 American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) recommendations, new evidence indicated that the health benefits significantly outweighed the drawbacks and complications of newborn male circumcisions. A reduction in urinary tract infections, sexually transmitted diseases, acquisition of HIV, reduced HPV, and penile cancer was evidenced. Circumcision was not associated with a decrease in sexual function or satisfaction. All families should be given the proper, complete, and unbiased objective information regarding circumcision. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) endorsed these statements. [32] [109] [110]

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Male circumcision is the surgical removal of the prepuce, or foreskin, covering the glans of the penis. Over the past decades, cultural changes and new research have led to a closer examination of the practice of circumcision. Although there is no definitive evidence regarding the impact of circumcision on sexual enjoyment, studies suggest a reduction in urinary tract infections among newborns. Neonatal circumcisions significantly reduce the risk of penile cancer later in life, but this benefit may be realized without surgery as the use of the human papillomavirus vaccine increases. Phimosis, paraphimosis, HIV infections, and balanitis are significantly reduced in adults who have been circumcised. The procedure remains controversial among individuals with conflicting cultural or religious beliefs.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Deterrence and Patient Education -- The Debate Over Circumcision
retrieved_docs.content: Getting a clear presentation of the facts in favor and against newborn circumcision can be difficult. The recent trend has been to do fewer circumcisions in the United States. However, according to the National Center for Health Statistics, about two-thirds of newborn males still receive the procedure. Pediatricians are often reluctant to perform the procedure as they tend to see the immediate adverse effects and complications (bleeding, infection, and pain), whereas urologists tend to recommend it as they see the late consequences in uncircumcised adults (balanitis, phimosis, paraphimosis, and penile cancer). Based on a recent extensive, evidence-based review of male circumcision, the relative benefits and drawbacks of the procedure are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance -- American Academy of Family Physicians
retrieved_docs.content: Family physicians should provide the family with complete information in an unbiased manner. Neonatal circumcision has potential health benefits in the reduction of urinary tract infections, sexually transmitted diseases, phimosis, paraphimosis, balanitis, and penile cancer. However, circumcision is not without complications. Now, the HPV vaccine alone may aid in the reduction of penile cancer without the need for circumcision.

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance -- American Urological Association
retrieved_docs.content: The circumcised newborn infant experiences the reasonable risks and complications associated with the procedure. During the first 3 to 6 months of life, the incidence of urinary tract infections is significantly higher in uncircumcised boys. Future risks of penile cancer, HIV and HPV infections, phimosis, paraphimosis, and balanitis are reduced—the circumcised adult benefits from these reduced health problems. The benefits and risks should be thoroughly discussed with the family.

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: No uniform opinion exists among clinicians on this issue. Pediatric and OB-GYN clinicians tend to be more reluctant to suggest neonatal circumcisions, as they are likely to observe their immediate associated side effects, problems, and complications. Urologists tend to recommend the procedure in neonates to avoid the need for medically necessary circumcisions in adulthood and to eliminate the risk of preventable complications, including some serious conditions such as balanitis, HIV infection, paraphimosis, and penile carcinoma caused by failing to perform the circumcision during infancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Deterrence and Patient Education -- Drawbacks
retrieved_docs.content: There is an estimation that it would take at least 1000 neonatal circumcisions or more to prevent 1 penile cancer. While this is likely, it is still unproven that the increased use of HPV vaccines will reduce the incidence of penile cancer even without circumcision. [90] [183] Neonatal deaths from circumcisions have been cited as another reason to avoid the procedure, but no actual mortality statistics from any official government agency or medical professional society document this as the incidence is too low. The overall risk-benefit analysis found that the proven benefits of circumcision far exceed the negatives by almost 200 to 1. [22] The American Academy of Pediatrics and the American Academy of Family Physicians agree that the benefits of circumcision far outweigh the risks. [184] However, this remains a controversial subject that is often decided by family circumcision history or religious practice. [185]

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance -- Benefits
retrieved_docs.content: The use of HPV vaccines has not yet been proven to reduce the future risk of penile cancer as well or as completely as neonatal circumcision.

retrieved_docs.title: Balanoposthitis -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: Another way to prevent balanoposthitis is circumcision. Although controversy exists regarding recommendations for circumcising neonates, research shows it prevents penile dermatosis, urinary tract infections, penile cancer, and sexually transmitted infections such as HIV, HPV, and syphilis. [69] [72] [73] See StatPearls' companion topic "Circumcision" for further information. [73]

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance -- Drawbacks
retrieved_docs.content: The health benefits of circumcision have been greatly overblown. At least 1000 or more neonatal circumcisions are needed to prevent 1 penile malignancy. The increased use of HPV vaccines is likely to reduce the future penile cancer risk, possibly as much as from circumcision.

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance -- Benefits
retrieved_docs.content: Strong evidence suggests that neonatal circumcisions eliminate the risk of penile cancer, which, though rare, are potentially lethal with high morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The interprofessional team includes a primary care doctor or advanced care clinician, a urologist, a radiologist, a nuclear medicine physician, an oncologist, and specialty-trained nurses. The team may also include pediatricians in their discussions regarding the pros and cons of neonatal circumcisions. Nurses assist in patient and family education, monitor patients, and report issues to the healthcare team. They also arrange close follow-ups when needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure should be delayed until the individual can decide for himself. However, delaying the procedure overlooks the fact that the procedure is a more significant surgery in adults and loses many known health benefits if conducted outside the neonatal period. Healthcare professionals may hold differing views on the benefits of circumcision and are likely to have individual opinions. Healthcare professionals help families with both the advantages and disadvantages of the procedure to make a well-informed decision. [45] [96] For example, HPV transmission can be reduced by circumcision. Clinicians must be educated about and understand religious doctrines, research findings, and cultural circumcision teachings. The data must be provided to each family in an unbiased manner. [88] [112] [113] [114] [115] [116] [117] [118] Clinicians must also be able to relate the data to any unique family situation.

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance -- Benefits
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of cervical cancer and sexually transmitted infections in future female sexual partners is reduced.

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance -- Benefits
retrieved_docs.content: Less exposure to HIV and HPV occurs.

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: For example, penile cancer is quite rare (1:100,000) but only found in men who did not undergo a neonatal circumcision. [102] [103] [104] When encountered, the 5-year relative mortality rate for penile cancer in the United States is 35%. Witnessing even one patient's preventable death due to penile cancer can prompt any urologist or oncologist to strongly advocate for neonatal circumcisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both listed as members of the Arabic‑named SMQ “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”, which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This classification indicates that, within this standardized medical terminology, these two distinct clinical categories are considered part of a broader collection of disorders that share underlying developmental, hereditary, or genetic characteristics. Recognizing their inclusion in the same SMQ can aid clinicians and researchers in organizing diagnostic codes, facilitating epidemiological studies, and ensuring comprehensive patient care that addresses both congenital urogenital conditions and infections transmitted through sexual contact under a unified framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1032
retrieved_docs.content: The category “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”—congenital, familial and genetic disorders—includes several distinct conditions. Genitourinary congenital anomalies are classified as members of this SMQ group, as are sexually transmitted infections, which are also considered part of the same broader classification. In addition, infantile cerebral palsy (فالج طفلي) and Stickle syndrome (متلازمة ستيكلر) are each listed as members of the SMQ category. By grouping these diverse entities under the umbrella of congenital, familial, and genetic disorders, the SMQ framework emphasizes their shared etiological themes and supports integrated clinical assessment and management.

Recognizing that other male genital disorders arise in the prostate, fall under the broader category of prostatic disorder, and are connected with التحصّي informs clinical assessment, diagnosis, and preventive care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both included within the SMQ titled الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ), which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This SMQ itself is classified as a member of the broader category فجل طفلي, indicating that these conditions are considered part of the larger spectrum of childhood developmental disorders. The hierarchical relationship underscores how specific anomalies and infections are organized under a unified genetic and congenital disorder framework, which in turn is situated within the context of pediatric developmental health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1519
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is linked to both the pathophysiology and the prevention and control of ولادة قبل الأوان (premature birth) and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية (fetal erythroblastosis). It also connects intestinal infection to the pathophysiology and to the prevention and control of بيلة سِّيسْتينِية (cystitis), عضال غدي (glandular tumor) and ورم الشفة (lip tumor). Moreover, the allowed qualifier is directly associated with the prevention and control of بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, عضال غدي and ورم الشفة. This integrated perspective underscores the role of the allowed qualifier in elucidating disease mechanisms and guiding preventive and control strategies across obstetric, hematologic, urinary and neoplastic conditions, while highlighting the relevance of intestinal infection in these contexts.
